column17: I didn't grow up in a family where there was anyone who was practicing in the healthcare space. While in my Form 4, my guardian was diagnosed with terminal stage 4 cancer and I'd frequently visit him in the hospital. I was really moved by what the oncology department and all the staffs generally on how their care was patient centered and empathetic and that ignited a flame within me that made me think that I'd love to help humans and alleviate their sufferings and make their terminal palliative care worth while. Having been good in Chemistry, Biology and Maths, I resonated to become a pharmacist, also, after having interacted with the clinical pharmacist who was making rounds in the oncology and I've never looked back since then!
column22: I think that conversations around the possibility to transition from one point of care to another i.e. for example BPHARM holders, the autonomy of shifting from clinical practice to non clinical practice and those who chose the clinical practice, the level of specialization and regulations based on the level of specialization. Also the prospects of job market so as to not churn out huge numbers of qualified practitioners saturating the job market yet a bigger percentage of the population remains unemployed.

column17: My decision to pursue medicine was not driven by external influence or formal research, but by a deep, personal conviction I have carried since early childhood. Choosing medicine is not something I arrived at through investigation ; it is something that has always felt inherent to who I am.
